title: Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System

(**IRNSS**), with an operational name of **NavIC** (acronym for **Navigation with Indian Constellation**; also, *nāvik* 'sailor' or 'navigator' in Indian languages),[[2]](#cite_note-2) is an autonomous regional [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") system that provides accurate real-time positioning, velocity and timing (PVT) services.[[3]](#cite_note-:17-3) It covers India and a region extending 1,500 km (930 mi) around it, with plans for further extension up to 3,000 km (1,900 mi) with 10–20 m (33–66 ft) position accuracy and 40ns time accuracy.[[4]](#cite_note-4) An extended service area lies between the primary service area and a rectangle area enclosed by the [30th parallel south](/wiki/30th_parallel_south "30th parallel south") to the [50th parallel north](/wiki/50th_parallel_north "50th parallel north") and the [30th meridian east](/wiki/30th_meridian_east "30th meridian east") to the [130th meridian east](/wiki/130th_meridian_east "130th meridian east"), 1,500–6,000 km (930–3,730 mi) beyond borders where some of the NavIC satellites are visible but the position is not always computable with assured accuracy.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

**Indian Regional Navigation Satellite System** (**IRNSS**), with an operational name of **NavIC** (acronym for **Navigation with Indian Constellation**; also, *nāvik* 'sailor' or 'navigator' in Indian languages),[[2]](#cite_note-2) is an autonomous regional [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") system that provides accurate real-time positioning, velocity and timing (PVT) services.[[3]](#cite_note-:17-3) It covers India and a region extending 1,500 km (930 mi) around it, with plans for further extension up to 3,000 km (1,900 mi) with 10–20 m (33–66 ft) position accuracy and 40ns time accuracy.[[4]](#cite_note-4) An extended service area lies between the primary service area and a rectangle area enclosed by the [30th parallel south](/wiki/30th_parallel_south "30th parallel south") to the [50th parallel north](/wiki/50th_parallel_north "50th parallel north") and the [30th meridian east](/wiki/30th_meridian_east "30th meridian east") to the [130th meridian east](/wiki/130th_meridian_east "130th meridian east"), 1,500–6,000 km (930–3,730 mi) beyond borders where some of the NavIC satellites are visible but the position is not always computable with assured accuracy.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

NavIC offers two levels of service, standard positioning service (SPS), which will be open for civilian use and is interoperable with [GNSS](/wiki/GNSS "GNSS"), and a restricted service (RS), an [encrypted](/wiki/Encryption "Encryption") channel for authorized users including the military, in L1 (1575.42 MHz, in use since the second generation), L5 (1176.45 MHz) and S Band (2498.028 MHz).[[6]](#cite_note-6) Originally designed as a seven satellite system with three atomic clocks each, which was achieved with the first generation of IRNSS satellite launches spanning 2013-2018, failure of on-board atomic clocks prompted ISRO to adopt indegenous atomic clocks, developed by [Space Application Centre, Ahmedabad](/wiki/Space_Applications_Centre "Space Applications Centre"), for its second generation of NVS satellites.[[7]](#cite_note-:26-7) Due to failure of on-board atomic clocks of the first generation satellites and launch delay of the second generation satellites, in March 2026, only three satellites of the constellation were providing navigation data, below a minimum number of four satellites required for an accurate position.[[8]](#cite_note-:28-8)

NavIC-based trackers are compulsory on commercial vehicles in India,[[9]](#cite_note-:9-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:10-10) and some consumer [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") with support for it have been available since the first half of 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

The system was developed partly because access to foreign government-controlled [global navigation satellite systems](/wiki/Global_navigation_satellite_system "Global navigation satellite system") is not guaranteed in hostile situations, as happened to the Indian military in 1999 when the United States denied an Indian request for [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) data for the [Kargil region](/wiki/Kargil_War "Kargil War"), which would have provided vital information.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The [Indian government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") approved the project in May 2006.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-:21-15)

The first generation (IRNSS series) of the satellite network was designed for 7 satellites, with [IRNSS-1A](/wiki/IRNSS-1A "IRNSS-1A"), [IRNSS-1B](/wiki/IRNSS-1B "IRNSS-1B"), [IRNSS-1C](/wiki/IRNSS-1C "IRNSS-1C"), [IRNSS-1D](/wiki/IRNSS-1D "IRNSS-1D"), [IRNSS-1E](/wiki/IRNSS-1E "IRNSS-1E"), [IRNSS-1F](/wiki/IRNSS-1F "IRNSS-1F") and [IRNSS-1G](/wiki/IRNSS-1G "IRNSS-1G") serving as initial set of satellites, and replacement satellites, [IRNSS-1H](/wiki/IRNSS-1H "IRNSS-1H") failing to reach orbit,[[16]](#cite_note-16) followed by successful launch of [IRNSS-1I](/wiki/IRNSS-1I "IRNSS-1I").[[17]](#cite_note-17) After continued failure of on-board atomic clocks of the first generation satellites, the second generation, utilizing mix of imported and indigenously developed atomic clocks and of increased operational lifetime from 10 to 12 years, are expected to support the network with 5 additional satellites, [NVS-01](/wiki/NVS-01 "NVS-01"), [NVS-02](/wiki/NVS-02 "NVS-02"), NVS-03, NVS-04 and NVS-05. However, failure of atomic clocks of IRNSS satellites, failure of NVS-02 to reach orbit and launch delay of NVS-03, NVS-04 and NVS-05 have contributed to only three of the satellites providing navigation data in March 2026, below the four minimum number of satellites required to provide reliable position.[[7]](#cite_note-:26-7)

## Developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Developments")]

### First Generation (IRNSS series)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First Generation (IRNSS series)")]

As part of the project, the [Indian Space Research Organisation](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation") (ISRO) opened a new satellite navigation centre within the campus of ISRO [Deep Space Network](/wiki/Indian_Deep_Space_Network "Indian Deep Space Network") (DSN) at [Byalalu](/wiki/Byalalu "Byalalu"), [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") on 28 May 2013.[[18]](#cite_note-18) A network of 21 ranging stations located across the country will provide data for the [orbital determination](/wiki/Orbit_determination "Orbit determination") of the satellites and monitoring of the navigation signal.

A goal of complete Indian control has been stated, with the space segment, [ground segment](/wiki/Ground_segment "Ground segment") and user receivers all being built in India. Its location in low [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") facilitates coverage with low-[inclination](/wiki/Orbital_inclination "Orbital inclination") satellites. Three satellites will be in [geostationary orbit](/wiki/Geostationary_orbit "Geostationary orbit") over the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"). Missile targeting could be an important military application for the constellation.[[19]](#cite_note-DN239-19)

The total cost of the project was expected to be [₹](/wiki/Indian_rupee "Indian rupee")14.2 billion (US$168 million), with the cost of the ground segment being ₹3 billion (US$35 million), each satellite costing ₹1.5 billion (US$18 million) and the PSLV-XL version rocket costing around ₹1.3 billion (US$15 million). The planned seven rockets would have involved an outlay of around ₹9.1 billion (US$108 million).[[20]](#cite_note-twostand-20)[[21]](#cite_note-dnai2j13-21)[[22]](#cite_note-ndtv2j13-22)

The necessity for two replacement satellites, and PSLV-XL launches, has altered the original budget, with the Comptroller and Auditor General of India reporting costs (as of March 2017) of ₹22.46 billion (US$266 million).[[1]](#cite_note-cag-1)

India's [Department of Space](/wiki/Department_of_Space "Department of Space") in their 12th Five Year Plan (FYP) (2012–17) stated increasing the number of satellites in the constellation from 7 to 11 to extend coverage.[[23]](#cite_note-:4-23) These additional four satellites will be made during 12th FYP and will be launched in the beginning of 13th FYP (2018–23) in [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") of 42° inclination.[[24]](#cite_note-:8-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Also, the development of space-qualified Indian made atomic clocks was initiated,[[26]](#cite_note-:18-26) along with a study and development initiative for an all [optical atomic clock](/wiki/Optical_clock "Optical clock") (ultra stable for IRNSS and [deep space communication](/wiki/Indian_Deep_Space_Network "Indian Deep Space Network")).[[27]](#cite_note-dst.gov.in-27)[[23]](#cite_note-:4-23)

The NavIC Signal in Space ICD was released for evaluation in September 2014.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

From 1 April 2019, use of [AIS 140](/wiki/Automotive_Industry_Standard_140 "Automotive Industry Standard 140") compliant NavIC-based vehicle tracking systems were made compulsory for all [commercial vehicles](/wiki/Commercial_vehicle "Commercial vehicle") in India.[[9]](#cite_note-:9-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:10-10)

In December 2019, the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") consented to designate NaVIC as one of their allied navigational satellite systems along with [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") (Europe) and [QZSS](/wiki/Quasi-Zenith_Satellite_System "Quasi-Zenith Satellite System") (Japan). The approval was as a part of [National Defense Authorization Act 2020](/wiki/National_Defense_Authorization_Act_for_Fiscal_Year_2020 "National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2020"). The proposal was put forward by [United States Secretary of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Defense "United States Secretary of Defense") in consultation with [Director of National Intelligence](/wiki/Director_of_National_Intelligence "Director of National Intelligence").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Clock failure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Clock failure")]

In 2017, it was announced that all three SpectraTime supplied [rubidium](/wiki/Rubidium "Rubidium") [atomic clocks](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock") on board [IRNSS-1A](/wiki/IRNSS-1A "IRNSS-1A") had failed, mirroring similar failures in the European Union's Galileo constellation.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The first failure occurred in July 2016, followed soon after by the two other clocks on IRNSS-1A. This rendered the satellite non-functional and required replacement.[[33]](#cite_note-33) ISRO reported it had replaced the atomic clocks in the two standby satellites, IRNSS-1H and IRNSS-1I in June 2017.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34) The subsequent launch of IRNSS-1H, as a replacement for IRNSS-1A, was unsuccessful when PSLV-C39 mission failed on 31 August 2017.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Degraded-35) The second standby satellite, IRNSS-1I, was successfully placed into orbit on 12 April 2018.[[36]](#cite_note-:6-36)

In July 2017, it was reported that two more clocks in the navigational system had also started showing signs of abnormality, thereby taking the total number of failed clocks to five,[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34) in May 2018 a failure of a further 4 clocks was reported, taking the count to 9 of the 24 in orbit.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

As a precaution to extend the operational life of navigation satellite, ISRO is running only one rubidium atomic clock instead of two in the remaining satellites.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-34)

As of May 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit) only four first generation satellites were capable of providing [navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") services[[38]](#cite_note-38) which is the minimum number required for service to remain operational.[[39]](#cite_note-NavIC_FAQ-39)

As of September 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit) only four satellites IRNSS-1B, IRNSS-1F, IRNSS-1I and NVS-01 were capable of providing [navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") services.[[40]](#cite_note-NavIC_Status_20241031-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In July 2025, while responding to a query through [Right to Information Act](/wiki/Right_to_Information_Act "Right to Information Act"), the ISRO revealed that five IRNSS satellites are completely defunct, with all of their three clocks having failed. Additionally, one satellite has only one functional clock and only two satellites are fully functional.[[42]](#cite_note-:24-42) In 2026, with IRNSS-1F clock failure marking completion of its 10 year mission life reduced the satellite network to only three operational satellites.[[8]](#cite_note-:28-8)

### Indian Atomic clock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Indian Atomic clock")]

In order to reduce the dependency on imported frequency standards ISRO's [Space Applications Centre](/wiki/Space_Applications_Centre "Space Applications Centre") (SAC), Ahmedabad had been working on domestically designed and developed [Rubidium based atomic clocks](/wiki/Rubidium_standard "Rubidium standard").[[3]](#cite_note-:17-3)[[27]](#cite_note-dst.gov.in-27)[[26]](#cite_note-:18-26)[[23]](#cite_note-:4-23) To overcome the clock failures on first generation navigation satellites and its subsequent impact on NavIC's [position, navigation, and timing](/wiki/Position,_navigation,_and_timing "Position, navigation, and timing") services, these new clocks would supplement the imported atomic clocks in next generation of navigation satellites.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-:16-46)

On 5 July 2017, ISRO and [Israel Space Agency](/wiki/Israel_Space_Agency "Israel Space Agency") (ISA) signed an [Memorandum of Understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") to collaborate on space qualifying a [Rubidium Standard](/wiki/Rubidium_standard "Rubidium standard") based on AccuBeat model AR133A and to test it on an ISRO satellite.[[47]](#cite_note-:19-47)

The clocks are utilised by the NVS series of satellites.[[15]](#cite_note-:21-15) As part of the **Times Dissemination Project**, which is overseen by the [Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food, and Public Distribution](/wiki/Ministry_of_Consumer_Affairs,_Food_and_Public_Distribution "Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food and Public Distribution"), NavIC will take the position of GPS as the reference time provider at the [National Physical Laboratory of India](/wiki/National_Physical_Laboratory_of_India "National Physical Laboratory of India") from 2025.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Microprocessor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Microprocessor")]

The [SPARC V8 ISA](/wiki/SPARC_V8 "SPARC V8") compatible AJIT microprocessor for robotics and industrial applications was created in 2018 by [IIT Bombay](/wiki/IIT_Bombay "IIT Bombay"). The [Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology](/wiki/Ministry_of_Electronics_and_Information_Technology "Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology"), IIT Bombay, and Powai Labs, a private firm from Mumbai, provided funding for the project. Initially produced by [Semi-Conductor Laboratory](/wiki/Semi-Conductor_Laboratory "Semi-Conductor Laboratory") utilizing 180nm process, commercial production using 65nm and 45nm are planned. AJIT operates at clock speeds between 70 and 120 MHz and can execute one instruction every clock cycle.

It includes a memory management unit that stores and retrieves data from memory, as well as an arithmetic logic unit that performs simple arithmetic and logical operations. Additionally, there is a floating point unit that can effectively manage computations involving non-integer quantities. A hardware debugger unit is used to monitor and control the microprocessor in order to program it. The [Society for Applied Microwave Electronics Engineering & Research](/wiki/Society_for_Applied_Microwave_Electronics_Engineering_%26_Research "Society for Applied Microwave Electronics Engineering & Research") will incorporate AJIT in the receivers being developed for NavIC.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-:25-51)

### NavIC chip

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: NavIC chip")]

In accordance with the range requirements for NavIC for both military and commercial applications, [Defence Research and Development Organisation](/wiki/Defence_Research_and_Development_Organisation "Defence Research and Development Organisation"), through the Technology Development Fund scheme, has commissioned Accord Software and Systems, to build a tailored and flexible IRNSS Network Timing system domestically. Using NavIC data, the receiver chip will obtain and distribute Indian time for navigation. India currently depends on the US for this service.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In 2020, [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") launched four Snapdragon [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") chipsets and one [5G](/wiki/5G "5G") chipset with support for NavIC.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) NavIC is planned to be available for civilian use in mobile devices, after Qualcomm and ISRO signed an agreement.[[55]](#cite_note-operational-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) To increase compatibility with existing hardware, ISRO will add L1 band support. For strategic application, Long Code support is also coming.[[57]](#cite_note-:15-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

On December 7, 2023, Qualcomm revealed that select chipset platforms will enable NavIC L1 signals. The Qualcomm location suite, supports up to seven satellite constellations simultaneously and allows for faster [Time to First Fix](/wiki/Time_to_first_fix "Time to first fix") (TTFF) position acquisition for enhanced [location-based services](/wiki/Location-based_service "Location-based service"). It also makes use of all of NavIC's L1 and L5 signals for precise positioning. In the second half of 2024, Qualcomm chipset platforms will add further support for the NavIC L1 signals, and in the first half of 2025, commercial products that support the NavIC L1 signals should be available for sale.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Timeline")]

In April 2010, it was reported that India plans to start launching satellites by the end of 2011, at a rate of one satellite every six months. This would have made NavIC functional by 2015. But the program was delayed,[[61]](#cite_note-61) and India also launched 3 new satellites to supplement this.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Seven satellites with the prefix "IRNSS-1" will constitute the space segment of the IRNSS. [IRNSS-1A](/wiki/IRNSS-1A "IRNSS-1A"), the first of the seven satellites, was launched on 1 July 2013.[[63]](#cite_note-isro-63)[[64]](#cite_note-businessline1-64) [IRNSS-1B](/wiki/IRNSS-1B "IRNSS-1B") was launched on 4 April 2014 on-board PSLV-C24 rocket. The satellite has been placed in [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit").[[65]](#cite_note-TOI-65) [IRNSS-1C](/wiki/IRNSS-1C "IRNSS-1C") was launched on 16 October 2014,[[66]](#cite_note-66) [IRNSS-1D](/wiki/IRNSS-1D "IRNSS-1D") on 28 March 2015,[[67]](#cite_note-67) [IRNSS-1E](/wiki/IRNSS-1E "IRNSS-1E") on 20 January 2016,[[68]](#cite_note-68) [IRNSS-1F](/wiki/IRNSS-1F "IRNSS-1F") on 10 March 2016 and [IRNSS-1G](/wiki/IRNSS-1G "IRNSS-1G") was launched on 28 April 2016.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The eighth satellite, [IRNSS-1H](/wiki/IRNSS-1H "IRNSS-1H"), which was meant to replace IRNSS-1A, failed to deploy on 31 August 2017 as the [heat shields](/wiki/Payload_fairing "Payload fairing") failed to separate from the 4th stage of the rocket.[[70]](#cite_note-:5-70) [IRNSS-1I](/wiki/IRNSS-1I "IRNSS-1I") was launched on 12 April 2018 to replace it.[[71]](#cite_note-IRNSS-1I-71)[[72]](#cite_note-irnss-1i-72) NVS-01, first of the second generation satellites, was successfully launched on 29 May 2023 and was placed into orbit.[[73]](#cite_note-73) NVS-02, launched on 29 January 2025, failed to reach orbit.[[74]](#cite_note-:27-74) NVS-03 was expected to launch in 2025 and NVS-04 and NVS-05 was expected to launch in 2026, however none of these have had launch schedules fixed.[[7]](#cite_note-:26-7)

## System description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: System description")]

The IRNSS system comprises a space segment and a support [ground segment](/wiki/Ground_segment "Ground segment").

### Space segment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Space segment")]

The constellation consists of 7 satellites. Three of the seven satellites are located in [geostationary orbit](/wiki/Geostationary_orbit "Geostationary orbit") (GEO) at [longitudes](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") 32.5° E, 83° E, and 131.5° E, approximately 36,000 km (22,000 mi) above Earth's surface. The remaining four satellites are in inclined [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit") (GSO). Two of them cross the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") at 55° E and two at 111.75° E.[[75]](#cite_note-:2-75)[[76]](#cite_note-ASM-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

### Ground segment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ground segment")]

The ground segment is responsible for the maintenance and operation of the IRNSS constellation. The ground segment comprises:[[75]](#cite_note-:2-75)

* IRNSS Spacecraft Control Facility (IRSCF)
* ISRO Navigation Centre (INC)
* IRNSS Range and Integrity Monitoring Stations (IRIMS)
* IRNSS Network Timing Centre (IRNWT)
* IRNSS CDMA Ranging Stations (IRCDR)
* Laser Ranging Stations
* IRNSS Data Communication Network (IRDCN)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/IRNSS_Series_1.jpg/250px-IRNSS_Series_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:IRNSS_Series_1.jpg)

Rendering of an IRNSS Series 1 satellite

The IRSCF is operational at Master Control Facility (MCF), Hassan and Bhopal. The MCF uplinks navigation data and is used for tracking, telemetry and command functions.[[78]](#cite_note-ar-2021-78) Seven 7.2-metre (24 ft) FCA and two 11-metre (36 ft) FMA of IRSCF are currently operational for [LEOP](/wiki/Launch_and_Early_Orbit_phase "Launch and Early Orbit phase") and on-orbit phases of IRNSS satellites.[[75]](#cite_note-:2-75)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The INC established at [Byalalu](/wiki/Byalalu "Byalalu") performs remote operations and data collection with all the ground stations. The ISRO Navigation Centers (INC) are operational at Byalalu, [Bengaluru](/wiki/Bengaluru "Bengaluru") and [Lucknow](/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow"). INC1 (Byalalu) and INC2 (Lucknow) together provide operations with [redundancy](/wiki/Redundancy_(engineering) "Redundancy (engineering)").[[80]](#cite_note-:11-80)

16 IRIMS are currently operational and are supporting IRNSS operations.[[81]](#cite_note-81) A few more are planned in Brunei, Indonesia, Australia, Russia, France and Japan.[[82]](#cite_note-82) [CDMA](/wiki/Code-division_multiple_access "Code-division multiple access") ranging is being carried out by the four IRCDR stations on a regular basis for all the IRNSS satellites. The IRNWT has been established and is providing IRNSS system time with an accuracy of 2 [ns](/wiki/Nanosecond "Nanosecond") (2.0×10−9 [s](/wiki/Second "Second")) (2 sigma) with respect to [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time"). Laser ranging is being carried out with the support of [ILRS](/wiki/Satellite_laser_ranging "Satellite laser ranging") stations around the world. Navigation software is operational at INC since 1 Aug 2013. All the navigation parameters, such as satellite [ephemeris](/wiki/Ephemeris "Ephemeris"), clock corrections, integrity parameters, and secondary parameters, such as iono-delay corrections, time offsets with respect to UTC and other [GNSSes](/wiki/GNSS "GNSS"), [almanac](/wiki/GPS_Almanac "GPS Almanac"), text message, and earth orientation parameters, are generated and uploaded to the spacecraft automatically. The IRDCN has established terrestrial and [VSAT](/wiki/Very-small-aperture_terminal "Very-small-aperture terminal") links between the ground stations. As of March 2021, ISRO and [JAXA](/wiki/JAXA "JAXA") are performing calibration and validation experiments for NavIC ground reference station in Japan.[[83]](#cite_note-83) ISRO is also under discussion with [CNES](/wiki/CNES "CNES") for a NavIC ground reference station in France.[[84]](#cite_note-84) ISRO is planning a NavIC ground station at [Cocos (Keeling) Islands](/wiki/Cocos_(Keeling)_Islands "Cocos (Keeling) Islands") and is in talks with the [Australian Space Agency](/wiki/Australian_Space_Agency "Australian Space Agency").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Signal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Signal")]

NavIC signals will consist of a Standard Positioning Service and a Restricted Service. Both will be carried on L5 (1176.45 MHz) and S band (2492.028 MHz).[[86]](#cite_note-86) The SPS signal will be modulated by a 1 MHz [BPSK](/wiki/QPSK "QPSK") signal. The Restricted Service will use [BOC(5,2)](/wiki/Binary_offset_carrier "Binary offset carrier"). The navigation signals themselves would be transmitted in the L5 (1176.45 MHz) & S band (2492.028 MHz) frequencies and broadcast through a phased array antenna to maintain required coverage and signal strength. The satellites would weigh approximately 1,330 kg (2,930 lb) and their solar panels generate 1,400 W.

A messaging interface is embedded in the NavIC system. This feature allows the command center to send warnings to a specific geographic area. For example, fishermen using the system can be warned about a cyclone.[[87]](#cite_note-:1-87)

### Accuracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Accuracy")]

The Standard Positioning Service system is intended to provide an absolute position accuracy of about 5 to 10 metres throughout the [Indian landmass](/wiki/Geography_of_India "Geography of India") and an accuracy of about 20 metres (66 ft) in the Indian Ocean as well as a region extending approximately 1,500 km (930 mi) around India.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) GPS, for comparison, has a position accuracy of 5 m under ideal conditions.[[90]](#cite_note-90) However, unlike GPS, which is dependent only on L-band, NavIC has dual frequencies (S and L bands). When a low-frequency signal travels through atmosphere, its velocity changes due to atmospheric disturbances. GPS depends on an atmospheric model to assess frequency error, and it has to update this model from time to time to assess the exact error. In NavIC, the actual delay is assessed by measuring the difference in delay of the two frequencies (S and L bands). Therefore, NavIC is not dependent on any model to find the frequency error and can be more accurate than GPS.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

## Future developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Future developments")]

### Second Generation (NVS series)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Second Generation (NVS series)")]

ISRO will be launching five next generation satellite featuring new payloads and extended lifespan of 12 years. Five new satellites viz. NVS-01, NVS-02, NVS-03, NVS-04 and NVS-05 will supplement and augment the current constellation of satellites. The new satellites will feature the L5 and S band and introduces a new interoperable civil signal in the [L1 band](/wiki/GPS_signals#L1C "GPS signals") in the navigation payload and will use Indian [Rubidium Atomic Frequency Standard](/wiki/Rubidium_standard "Rubidium standard") (iRAFS.)[[46]](#cite_note-:16-46)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-:12-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) This introduction of the new L1 band will help facilitate NavIC proliferation in [wearable smart](/wiki/Wearable_technology "Wearable technology") and [IoT](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") devices featuring a low power navigation system. [NVS-01](/wiki/NVS-01 "NVS-01") is a replacement for [IRNSS-1G](/wiki/IRNSS-1G "IRNSS-1G") satellite and was launched on [GSLV](/wiki/Geosynchronous_Satellite_Launch_Vehicle "Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle") in 2023.[[95]](#cite_note-:7-95)[[78]](#cite_note-ar-2021-78)[[96]](#cite_note-TR-290523-96)

ISRO has plans for a total of 7 NVS series satellites (including already launched NVS-1) for civilian navigation requirements. The IRNSS network is, as of November 2024, confined to strategic use by the [Indian Armed Forces](/wiki/Indian_Armed_Forces "Indian Armed Forces"). They will be equipped with L1 band along with the L5 and S band. The system will provide an accuracy of 10 m (33 ft) within India, 20 m (66 ft) for the area surrounding India by 1,500 km (930 mi).[[97]](#cite_note-:20-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)

Approved project cost of first five NVS satellites (NVS-01 to NVS-05) is ₹964.68 crore (equivalent to ₹10 billion or US$120 million in 2023) excluding the launch costs.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

As reported in August 2025, ISRO plans to launch at least three satellites by the end of 2026. However, the development of indigenous atomic clocks is an "element impeding the launch". The development is also being delayed since multiple components are needed to be imported which leads to procurement challenges. Following the multiple instances of atomic clock failures, five atomic clocks per satellite has been proposed for future units.[[42]](#cite_note-:24-42)

### Global Indian Navigation System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Global Indian Navigation System")]

Study and analysis for the Global Indian Navigation System (GINS) was initiated as part of the technology and policy initiatives in the 12th FYP (2012–17).[[27]](#cite_note-dst.gov.in-27) The system is supposed to have a constellation of 24 satellites, positioned 24,000 km (14,913 mi) above Earth. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_Regional_Navigation_Satellite_System&action=edit), the [statutory filing](/wiki/Statutory "Statutory") for [frequency spectrum](/wiki/Frequency_spectrum "Frequency spectrum") of GINS satellite orbits in international space, has been completed.[[100]](#cite_note-100) As per new 2021 draft policy,[[101]](#cite_note-101) ISRO and [Department of Space](/wiki/Department_of_Space "Department of Space") (DoS) is working on expanding the coverage of NavIC from regional to global that will be independent of other such system currently operational namely [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"), [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS"), [BeiDou](/wiki/BeiDou "BeiDou") and [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") while remaining interoperable and free for global public use.[[102]](#cite_note-102) ISRO has proposed to Government of India to expand the constellation for global coverage by initially placing twelve satellites in [Medium Earth Orbit](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") (MEO).[[57]](#cite_note-:15-57)
